author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
The Misrepresentation of the Pelagians Concerning the Use of the Old Law.
title: The Misrepresentation of the Pelagians Concerning the Use of the Old Law.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Chapter 2 [II.]—The Misrepresentation of the Pelagians Concerning the Use of the Old Law.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: They declare “that we say that the law of the Old Testament was given not for the end that it might justify the obedient, but rather that it might become the cause of greater sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Certainly, they do not understand what we say concerning the law; because we say what the apostle says, whom they do not understand.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For who can say that they are not justified who are obedient to the law, when, unless they were justified, they could not be obedient?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But we say, that by the law is effected that what God wills to be done is heard, but that by grace is effected that the law is obeyed.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For not the hearers of the law,” says the apostle, “are just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore the law makes hearers of righteousness, grace makes doers.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For what was impossible to the law,” says the same apostle, “in that it was weak through the flesh, God sent His Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin condemned sin in the flesh: that the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us who walk not according to the flesh, but according to the Spirit.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This is what we say;—let them pray that they may one day understand it, and not dispute so as never to understand it.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For it is impossible that the law should be fulfilled by the flesh, that is, by carnal presumption, in which the proud, who are ignorant of the righteousness of God,—that is, which is of God to man, that he may be righteous,—and desirous of establishing their own righteousness,—as if by their own will, unassisted from above, the law could be fulfilled,—are not subjected to the righteousness of God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore the righteousness of the law is fulfilled in them who walk not according to the flesh—that is, according to man, ignorant of the righteousness of God and desirous of establishing his own—but walk according to the Spirit.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But who walks according to the Spirit, except whosoever is led by the Spirit of God?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, these are the sons of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore “the letter killeth, but the Spirit maketh alive.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And the letter is not evil because it killeth; but it convicts the wicked of transgression.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For the law is holy, and the commandment holy and just and good.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Was, then,” says he, “that which is good made death unto me?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: By no means; but sin, that it might appear sin, worked death in me by that which is good, that it might become above measure a sinner or a sin by the commandment.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This is what is the meaning of “the letter killeth.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: “For the sting of death is sin, but the strength of sin is the law;” because by the prohibition it increases the desires of sin, and thence slays a man unless grace by coming to his assistance makes him alive.
